era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant political, technological, and cultural transformation across multiple civilisations. In Europe, the Renaissance is flourishing in Italy, and centralised nation-states are consolidating power. The Ming Dynasty in China is at its cultural and administrative peak. The Ottoman Empire is expanding into the Balkans. In South America, the Inca Empire is undergoing rapid territorial expansion. This era is marked by technological innovation, increased maritime exploration, and the beginning of European global expansion.

events.title: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui ascends to power; beginning of Inca imperial expansion
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui ascended to power as the ninth Inca ruler in 1438 (some sources date this to 1440). His accession marked the beginning of a dramatic transformation of the Inca state from a regional power into a vast empire. Pachacuti initiated a series of military campaigns that expanded Inca territory from the Cusco region to encompass much of the Andes, including present-day Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, and parts of Chile and Argentina. He also reorganised the Inca administrative system, introducing the mit'a labour system and the quipu record-keeping system on a larger scale. Pachacuti's reign (traditionally dated 1438–1471) is considered the foundation of the Inca Empire as a major imperial power. His military and administrative innovations transformed the Inca state into one of the largest empires in pre-Columbian America.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th century
Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (Chronicle of Peru), written 1550s
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas, written early 17th century
Archaeological evidence from Inca administrative sites and architecture

events.certainty_note: The Inca did not use a written calendar system. Dates for Inca events are reconstructed from oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades or centuries later. Pachacuti's accession is traditionally dated to 1438, though some sources prefer 1440. The date 1438 is widely accepted in modern scholarship, but uncertainty remains.

events.title: Council of Florence begins; attempt to reunite Eastern and Western Christian churches
events.description: The Council of Florence began in 1438 as a major ecumenical council of the Catholic Church. The council was convened to address the schism between the Eastern Orthodox Church and the Roman Catholic Church, which had been in schism since 1054. The council brought together representatives of both churches, including Pope Eugenius IV, the Patriarch of Constantinople, and the Byzantine Emperor John VIII Palaiologus. The council would continue until 1445 and would reach a temporary agreement on the union of the churches in 1439. The council is significant as an attempt at Christian reconciliation and as a reflection of the broader intellectual and diplomatic currents of the Renaissance.
key_figures: Pope Eugenius IV (Roman Catholic Pope, r. 1431–1447)
Patriarch Joseph II of Constantinople (Eastern Orthodox Patriarch)
Emperor John VIII Palaiologus (Byzantine Emperor, r. 1425–1448)

sources.name: Council of Florence official records and decrees

events.certainty_note: The Council of Florence's beginning in 1438 is well-documented in papal, conciliar, and Orthodox church records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Led to 1453-001 (Ottoman conquest of Constantinople; union repudiated)

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration; continued expansion along the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued their systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1438. Portuguese ships were gradually pushing further south along the coast, establishing trading posts and gathering information about African geography, peoples, and resources. This period saw the beginning of Portuguese commercial interest in African gold, ivory, and other goods. The exploration was motivated by a combination of factors: the desire for wealth, the spread of Christianity, and the accumulation of geographical knowledge. These voyages laid the groundwork for the later Portuguese slave trade and European colonialism in Africa.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and administrative documents
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica dos Feitos da Guiné (Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea), written c. 1453–1460

events.certainty_note: Portuguese maritime exploration along the African coast was ongoing throughout the 1430s–1440s. Specific events in 1438 are less well-documented than the major raids and discoveries of 1441 and later years.
cross_references: Led to 1441-001 (Portuguese slave raid at Cape Bojador)

events.title: Ming Dynasty; Emperor Yingzong's reign; cultural and administrative consolidation
events.description: Emperor Yingzong ascended to the Ming throne in 1435 and continued his reign in 1438. His reign (r. 1435–1449, 1457–1464) was marked by cultural and administrative consolidation. The Ming Dynasty was at its height during this period, with a strong centralised government, a flourishing economy, and significant cultural achievements in art, literature, and architecture. However, Yingzong's reign also saw the gradual withdrawal of support for maritime exploration, as Confucian officials increasingly opposed the treasure voyages as wasteful. The Ming court was focused on internal consolidation and defence against Mongol incursions from the north.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records

events.certainty_note: Emperor Yingzong's reign and the general characteristics of the Ming Dynasty during this period are well-documented in Ming court records.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming Dynasty history (1368–1644)

events.title: Ottoman Empire; Murad II's military campaigns in the Balkans
events.description: Under Sultan Murad II (r. 1421–1444, 1446–1451), the Ottoman Empire continued its systematic conquest and consolidation of the Balkans in 1438. Ottoman forces were engaged in campaigns against Hungarian and Venetian strongholds in the region. The Ottomans were gradually eliminating Christian resistance and establishing Ottoman administrative control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. This period saw the transformation of the Ottoman state from a regional Anatolian power into a major Balkan and Mediterranean power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans during the 1430s–1440s are well-documented, though specific events in 1438 are less detailed than those of 1440–1441.
cross_references: Part of broader Ottoman expansion in the 15th century

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Pachacuti's accession (1438-001) is debated; some sources prefer 1440. The uncertainty reflects the absence of written Inca records and the reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers. The Council of Florence (1438-002) is sometimes presented as a major success in Christian reconciliation, but the union was rejected by many Orthodox Christians and was short-lived. Portuguese maritime exploration (1438-003) was ongoing but less dramatic than the major raids and discoveries of 1441 and later years.
historiographic_note: The year 1438 is well-documented for European, Ottoman, and East Asian events, reflecting the bias of surviving sources toward literate, centralised states. Inca history relies entirely on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th century, introducing significant uncertainty about precise dating and interpretation. African, indigenous American, and many Asian societies' perspectives on these events are largely absent from surviving sources. The historical record for 1438 is shaped by the priorities and biases of European, Ottoman, and Chinese chroniclers.

graph_edges.note: Pachacuti's accession initiated the rapid Inca expansion, including the conquest of Chincha in 1441.
The Council of Florence (1438–1445) reached the Decree of Union in 1439.
Portuguese maritime exploration led to the slave raid at Cape Bojador in 1441.
Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans continued in 1439–1441.
